title: Statistical Inference and Hypothesis Testing
description: Master the fundamentals of hypothesis testing: from setting up null and alternative hypotheses to interpreting p-values and making statistically sound decisions.

nodes.title: What is Hypothesis Testing?
nodes.content: Hypothesis testing is a statistical method for making decisions about a population based on sample data. We start with a null hypothesis (H₀), which represents no effect or relationship, and an alternative hypothesis (H₁), which represents the effect we're testing. The test helps us determine if our sample provides enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis. This framework is fundamental to empirical research across science, medicine, and business.

nodes.title: Setting Up Null and Alternative Hypotheses
nodes.content: A researcher wants to know if a new teaching method improves test scores compared to the current baseline of 75. They formulate: H₀: μ = 75 (no improvement) and H₁: μ > 75 (improvement exists). This is a one-tailed test because they only care about improvement. They collect data from 50 students and set a significance level α = 0.05, meaning they'll tolerate a 5% chance of falsely rejecting the null hypothesis.

nodes.title: Hypothesis Setup Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of hypothesis formulation and error types.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical company tests if a new drug reduces blood pressure from a baseline of 140 mmHg. Which represents the correct hypotheses?
feedback: A two-tailed test is used when we don't know the direction of change. Here we expect reduction in blood pressure.
The null hypothesis should reflect 'no change' from the baseline value.
Correct! H₀ states no reduction; H₁ states the reduction we're testing for.
The null hypothesis doesn't state an inequality; it represents the baseline condition.
questions.explanation: In hypothesis testing, H₀ always states no effect or the baseline value, while H₁ states the direction or magnitude of the hypothesized change. Since we specifically expect reduction, this is a one-tailed test.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Type I error in hypothesis testing?
options: Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true
Failing to reject H₀ when it is actually false
Having a sample size that is too small
feedback: Correct! A Type I error is rejecting a true null hypothesis (false positive).
This describes a Type II error, which is a false negative.
This is the correct decision in hypothesis testing.
Sample size affects statistical power but is not an error type.
questions.explanation: Type I error (α) is the probability of falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis; Type II error (β) is the probability of failing to reject a false null hypothesis. Researchers control α through the predetermined significance level.

nodes.title: Understanding P-values
nodes.content: A p-value is the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme or more extreme than the one calculated, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A smaller p-value provides stronger evidence against H₀. We compare the p-value to a predetermined significance level (α), typically 0.05. The decision rule is straightforward: if p < α, we reject H₀; otherwise, we fail to reject it. The p-value does not tell us the probability that H₀ is true.

nodes.title: P-value Interpretation in Practice
nodes.content: A teacher wonders if their class's average test score differs from the school's long-standing average of 80 points. After testing 30 students, they calculate t = 2.15 with a p-value = 0.042. Since p = 0.042 < 0.05 (the predetermined significance level), they reject H₀ and conclude the class mean differs significantly from 80. Note: statistical significance doesn't guarantee practical importance—a small difference might be statistically significant but educationally trivial.

nodes.title: P-value Decision Assessment
nodes.content: Test your ability to interpret p-values and make appropriate statistical decisions.

questions.question: In a study testing if exercise improves mood, the researcher obtains p = 0.08 with α = 0.05. What conclusion is appropriate?
options: Reject H₀; exercise definitely improves mood
Fail to reject H₀; there is not enough evidence to conclude exercise improves mood
Accept H₀; exercise does not affect mood
Collect more data because the p-value is close to α
feedback: A p-value > α means we do not have sufficient evidence to reject H₀.
Correct! When p > α, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Using 'accept' is incorrect terminology in hypothesis testing; we 'fail to reject.'
While data collection could be beneficial, the immediate decision is to fail to reject H₀.
questions.explanation: The significance level α = 0.05 is a predetermined threshold. If p ≥ α, we fail to reject H₀, regardless of how close the p-value is to the threshold. This protects against Type I errors.

questions.question: What does it mean if a hypothesis test has a p-value of 0.002?
options: There is a 0.2% chance the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing the data (or more extreme) given H₀ is true is 0.2%
There is a 99.8% chance the alternative hypothesis is true
The sample mean is definitely different from the population mean
feedback: The p-value does not directly give the probability that H₀ is true.
Correct! This is the precise definition of a p-value: P(data | H₀).
We cannot calculate the probability of H₁ being true from the p-value.
A small p-value suggests a difference but doesn't guarantee practical significance.
questions.explanation: A p-value is a conditional probability: P(data|H₀), not P(H₀|data). It measures how extreme the observed data would be if the null hypothesis were true. Smaller p-values indicate stronger evidence against H₀.

nodes.title: Hypothesis Testing Practice
nodes.content: Strengthen your skills with practice problems that integrate hypothesis setup and p-value interpretation.

questions.question: A company claims their batteries last 500 hours on average. A consumer group tests 25 batteries and finds a mean of 485 hours. Which hypothesis setup is correct?
feedback: Correct! The company's claim is the baseline (H₀), and we test if it's lower than claimed.
Hypotheses cannot be reversed like this.
The null hypothesis represents the company's claim, not the alternative.
Both hypotheses should use the same parameter: the population mean.
questions.explanation: We always set H₀ to the baseline or claim being tested. Since consumers suspect shorter life, H₁ is one-tailed: μ < 500.

questions.question: If the calculated p-value is 0.035 and the significance level is α = 0.05, what decision should be made?
feedback: The p-value (0.035) is less than α (0.05), so we reject H₀.
Correct! Since p < α, we reject the null hypothesis.
With a defined α, we have a clear decision rule.
The decision rule is predetermined; we don't make data collection decisions post-hoc.
questions.explanation: The decision rule is simple: if p < α, reject H₀. Here, 0.035 < 0.05, so we reject the null hypothesis and conclude there is sufficient evidence of an effect.

nodes.title: Choose Your Next Step
nodes.content: You've successfully mastered the fundamentals of hypothesis testing! You now understand how to set up hypotheses, conduct tests, and interpret p-values. Would you like to review the concepts, or complete the course?

nodes.title: Course Complete!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've completed the Statistical Inference and Hypothesis Testing course. You now understand the core principles of hypothesis testing: formulating null and alternative hypotheses, conducting statistical tests, interpreting p-values, and making informed decisions based on evidence. These skills form the foundation for critical thinking in research, data analysis, and decision-making across disciplines.
